topic: end of year teacher appreciation — feels awkward

turns.content: Teacher Appreciation Week is coming up at my school, and I am already dreading it. I realize that makes me sound ungrateful, but I wonder if you can help me think through why it makes me so uncomfortable.

You don't sound ungrateful at all -- that's actually a fairly common reaction among teachers, especially experienced ones. There's often a disconnect between how appreciation is expressed and how it's received.

What specifically makes you uncomfortable about it? Is it the public attention, the nature of the gestures, the feeling that it's performative, or something else?

All of those, to varying degrees. The public attention is certainly part of it -- I am not someone who enjoys being the center of a room's focus unless I am teaching, which is a different dynamic entirely. But I think what bothers me most is the performative quality. The administration spends a week distributing gift cards and putting up banners, and then the other fifty-one weeks of the year they are increasing class sizes and adding paperwork. It feels less like genuine appreciation and more like a pressure valve.

That's a perceptive critique, and I think you're identifying something real. There's a term in organizational psychology -- "recognition in lieu of resources" -- where symbolic gestures substitute for substantive support. A $10 coffee gift card doesn't address the systemic issues you're describing.

It's a bit like how ancient Roman leaders would throw festivals and distribute bread during times of genuine civic neglect. The spectacle serves a function, but it's not the function it claims to serve.

That said, I'd separate the institutional performance from the individual gestures. Do students or parents ever express appreciation during the week in ways that feel more genuine?

You are absolutely right to make that distinction, and the Roman parallel is apt -- panem et circenses, but for educators. The institutional version is what grates. The student gestures are different. I do occasionally get a handwritten note from a student, and those are genuinely moving. Last year a former student who is now in college sent me an email saying that my class was the reason she chose to study international relations. That meant more to me than any banner in the faculty lounge.

That email is worth more than a decade of gift cards, and the fact that you recognize the difference speaks well of you. The handwritten notes and the emails from former students -- those are expressions of actual impact. They're specific, personal, and connected to something real you did.

The institutional version, by contrast, is generic by design. It has to be, because it's not really about individual teachers -- it's about the institution performing the act of appreciation. And there's an inherent awkwardness in being the object of a performance you can see through.

Do you feel pressure to participate enthusiastically? To show up at events, accept the gestures graciously, perform gratitude in return?

There is absolutely that expectation, yes. There is a luncheon, and attendance is technically optional in the way that most things in institutional life are technically optional -- which is to say, not at all. I will attend. I will smile. I will eat the catered sandwich. But there is a particular kind of fatigue that comes from performing appreciation for being appreciated.

"Performing appreciation for being appreciated" -- that's a beautifully precise way to describe the exhaustion of that social contract. You're essentially being asked to do emotional labor in response to what is itself a form of emotional labor on the administration's part. Everyone is performing, and everyone knows everyone is performing, and yet the show must go on.

I think the healthiest approach might be to treat the institutional parts as exactly what they are -- a social ritual you participate in because you're part of a community, not because it nourishes you. Go to the luncheon, eat the sandwich, make small talk, and don't feel guilty about finding it hollow. You're not obligated to be moved by a catered lunch.

And then guard the things that do nourish you -- the notes, the emails, the moments in class when you can see a student genuinely thinking for the first time. Those are your actual appreciation.

That is a healthy framing. Treat it as ritual rather than expecting it to be meaningful. There is a historian's comfort in that, actually -- I spend my professional life studying rituals and ceremonies that served social functions quite separate from their stated purposes. I should be able to extend that analytical generosity to a teacher appreciation luncheon.

Exactly. And there's something almost freeing about that perspective. Once you stop expecting the luncheon to be meaningful and start seeing it as a social ritual -- not unlike a diplomatic reception or a state dinner -- the pressure to feel something authentic evaporates. You're a participant-observer, fulfilling a role in a communal ceremony.

The irony, of course, is that a history teacher is uniquely equipped to understand that most expressions of institutional gratitude throughout human civilization have been more about social cohesion than genuine sentiment. You've probably taught lessons about exactly this kind of dynamic.

I have, many times. The Triumph in Roman culture, the medieval feast, the Soviet May Day parade -- all rituals that served the institution far more than the individuals being ostensibly honored. I suppose I should add "Teacher Appreciation Week luncheon" to that pedagogical list. Though I will keep that observation to myself at the actual event.

That's probably wise. Comparing the luncheon to a Soviet May Day parade, however accurate it might feel, is unlikely to endear you to the principal.

But privately, I think you've arrived at exactly the right place: attend with grace, accept with courtesy, find the humor in it, and save your emotional energy for the interactions that actually matter. The catered sandwich is just a sandwich. The email from a former student who changed her major because of your class -- that's the real thing.

You'll survive the week. You always do.

I do wonder sometimes if the younger teachers find it less grating, or if this is a universal reaction that simply intensifies with experience. Some of my newer colleagues seem genuinely delighted by the whole affair, and I cannot tell if that is sincere enthusiasm or if they are simply better at the performance than I am.

That's an interesting question, and I suspect the answer is a bit of both. For newer teachers, the appreciation may still feel novel -- they haven't yet accumulated enough luncheons to see the pattern. When you've been through something once or twice, the gesture can feel fresh. By the fifteenth or twentieth iteration, the formula becomes visible.

There's also a practical element: early-career teachers are often still in a phase where institutional validation matters more, because they're still establishing themselves. Once you've been teaching long enough to know your own value independent of what the administration says during a designated week, the external recognition loses some of its weight.

But I wouldn't assume their enthusiasm is entirely performative. They may genuinely enjoy the community aspect of it -- the gathering, the collective identity, the sense of being seen. Those needs don't disappear with experience, but the specific ritual may stop satisfying them.

I have survived more than twenty of them at this point. One more will not break me. Thank you -- this was a useful conversation. Sometimes it helps simply to articulate why something bothers you rather than letting the irritation sit unexamined. I will attend the luncheon with the serene detachment of an anthropologist conducting fieldwork.

That is the perfect mindset. Serene anthropological detachment, a catered sandwich, and the quiet knowledge that your actual impact on students' lives has nothing to do with a banner in the faculty lounge. You'll be fine.

And for what it's worth -- the fact that you find genuine appreciation uncomfortable and performative appreciation irritating suggests your motivations for teaching have always been in the right place. People who do the work for the recognition tend to love Appreciation Week. People who do it for the work itself tend to find it slightly absurd.

Enjoy your fieldwork, David.
